vocabulary.def: A program that learns patterns from examples rather than following fixed instructions
The examples the AI learns from
A rule or regularity the AI identifies across many examples
When training data is incomplete or unbalanced, causing the AI to make unfair or wrong decisions
The AI's best guess based on patterns it has seen before

lessonSlides.title: Get the Room Ready
lessonSlides.roleModel: Fei-Fei Li (born 1976) is a computer scientist and professor at Stanford University who built ImageNet — a database of over 14 million labelled images that became the foundation for most modern AI image recognition. Before ImageNet, AI could not reliably identify a cat in a photo. After training on her data, it could. She showed that the secret to better AI was not a smarter algorithm — it was better, more diverse training data.
whatToDo: Write these five words on the board and leave them visible all lesson:
Artificial Intelligence (AI): a program that learns patterns from examples, not fixed rules
Training Data: the examples the AI learns from
Pattern: a rule or regularity the AI finds across many examples
Bias: when incomplete training data causes unfair or wrong decisions
Prediction: the AI's best guess based on patterns it has seen before

Prepare 12–16 picture cards — print or hand-draw mixed objects, animals, and people (suggestions: dog, cat, horse, bird, fish, apple, banana, chair, car, bicycle, person running, person sitting, tree, cloud).
Decide your secret rule for the warm-up. Good options: YES to all animals, YES to things that can fly, YES to things with four legs, or YES to things found outside.
Make sure you have at least one surprising card to force a model revision — e.g. a chair if your rule is 'four legs.'
lessonSlides.tip: Keep all picture cards face-down until Phase 2. The reveal works best when students have no preview of what's coming.

lessonSlides.title: Spark Question — How Does Your Phone Know Your Face?
lessonSlides.whatToSay: Your phone can unlock by recognising your face. It has never taken a class in faces. Nobody programmed it with a list of rules about what your nose looks like. So how does it know it is you?
whatToDo: Ask students to turn to the person next to them and share one idea.
Take 3–4 answers and write the most interesting ones on the board.

Then say: 'The answer is not that it is clever. The answer is that it has seen thousands of photos of your face from different angles, in different lighting, with different expressions — and it found the pattern.'

Ask: 'Has an app ever recommended something surprisingly right — like it somehow knew exactly what you wanted?' Then: 'Has it ever recommended something completely wrong?' Tell students both happen because of patterns in training data.
lessonSlides.tip: Students often say 'it memorised your face' or 'it scanned you.' Both point in the right direction. Redirect toward the word pattern. A scanner follows rules; AI finds its own rules from examples. That distinction is the conceptual core of this lesson.

lessonSlides.title: Warm-Up — You Are the AI
lessonSlides.whatToSay: I have a secret rule in my head. I am going to show you some cards one at a time and say either YES or NO. Your job is to figure out my rule — just like an AI figuring out a pattern from training data. Do not shout out your guess. Write it down when you think you know.
whatToDo: Hold up picture cards one at a time. Say YES or NO according to your secret rule.
After 8–10 cards, ask: 'How many of you think you know the rule? Don't say it yet — write it down.'
Show 2–3 more cards to confirm or challenge their hypothesis.
Take 3–4 guesses. Reveal the actual rule.
Ask: 'How many examples did you need before you were confident? Did anyone change their guess after seeing a new card that didn't fit?'
Ask: 'What if I had only shown you the first three cards? Would you have been as confident? What if those three were all very similar — what patterns might you miss?'
lessonSlides.tip: Include at least one surprising card to force model revision — e.g. if your rule is 'four legs,' include a table or chair. When students see a chair get a YES, their hypothesis breaks and they revise it. Name that moment explicitly: 'What just happened to your theory when you saw the chair? That is called updating the model — and AI does exactly that every time it sees a new example.'

lessonSlides.title: Concept Introduction — Traditional Programming vs Machine Learning
lessonSlides.whatToSay: For the last four weeks, every program we wrote followed fixed instructions. A sequence runs in order. A loop repeats. A conditional checks a rule. A variable stores a value. All of that is traditional programming — a human writes the rules and the computer follows them. AI is different. In AI, the human does not write the rules. Instead, the human gives the computer thousands of examples — and the computer figures out the rules itself.
whatToDo: Write on the board: TRADITIONAL PROGRAMMING: Human writes rules → Computer follows rules → Output
Write underneath: MACHINE LEARNING (a type of AI): Human gives examples → Computer finds rules → Output

Give the cat example: 'You want to teach an AI to recognise cats. You don't write rules. You show it one million photos labelled cat and one million labelled not cat. The AI finds the patterns itself.'
Ask: 'What is the AI doing when it makes a prediction?' — it is checking whether a new photo matches patterns from training data. It is running a very complex conditional — but one it wrote itself.
Introduce bias: 'What if all one million cat photos were taken in sunny California in the daytime? Then someone shows it a cat in the dark, or in the snow, or photographed from above — what happens?'
Write on the board: BETTER TRAINING DATA → BETTER AI | INCOMPLETE TRAINING DATA → BIASED AI
Connect back to the warm-up: 'If I had shown you only outdoor-animal cards, you might have guessed the wrong rule. That is training data bias happening in real time.'
lessonSlides.realWorldConnection: Fei-Fei Li's insight was that AI image recognition was bad not because the algorithms were weak, but because the training data was too small and too narrow. When she built ImageNet with 14 million diverse images, AI accuracy jumped from roughly 70% to over 95% in just a few years. The data was the breakthrough, not the algorithm.
lessonSlides.tip: When you write the two programming models on the board, leave them there for the rest of the lesson. Students will refer back to them during exercises.

lessonSlides.title: Train the Class Recommender
lessonSlides.whatToSay: You are going to train a class AI to predict what kind of content a student likes to watch. I am the student. You are the AI — and you start knowing nothing about me.
whatToDo: Draw two columns on the board: WATCH ✓  |  SKIP ✗
Read out these 12 titles and sort them yourself — do not explain why:
Minecraft Let's Play → WATCH
A cooking tutorial → SKIP
Dog learns to skateboard → WATCH
Football match highlights → SKIP
Cat knocks things off a table → WATCH
How to bake a cake → SKIP
Goat climbs a tree → WATCH
Basketball trick shots → SKIP
Parrot sings a song → WATCH
Travel vlog in Japan → SKIP
Hamster runs a maze → WATCH
Science experiment explosion → SKIP
After all 12, ask pairs to study the pattern and write their prediction of the rule. Take answers — correct rule: 'animals doing funny or unusual things.'
Then test on three new titles: 'Horse learns to open a gate' (WATCH), 'How to make pizza' (SKIP), 'Penguin steals a fish from a market' (WATCH). Students predict, then you confirm.
lessonSlides.tip: After the reveal, say explicitly: 'You just did what a recommendation AI does. You found the pattern in the training data and used it to make predictions about new content.'
lessonSlides.roleModelMoment: Fei-Fei Li trained AI on 14 million labelled examples — one at a time. You are doing exactly that right now: giving the machine labelled examples to learn from.

lessonSlides.title: Find the Bias
lessonSlides.whatToSay: Here is a new title: 'Man runs a marathon in world record time.' WATCH or SKIP?
whatToDo: Students say SKIP — no animal. Confirm: SKIP.

Say: 'A year later, the student gets a dog and starts running with it. Now marathon videos are suddenly interesting. But the AI still predicts SKIP — because its training data is from a year ago.' Ask: 'What is the problem?' (Training data is outdated.)
Introduce demographic bias: 'The same AI is used for all students in a school. But the training data came only from 11-year-old boys. A 9-year-old girl starts using the app. What might go wrong?'
Students suggest: recommendations won't match her. Say: 'That is demographic bias — the training data did not represent all types of people who use the system.'
Ask pairs to write down two things that would make this AI better. Accept: more diverse data, more recent data, letting users correct wrong recommendations — all are real techniques.
lessonSlides.tip: Write 'outdated data' and 'demographic bias' on the board as two named types of bias. Naming them precisely is the skill the exercise is testing.
lessonSlides.roleModelMoment: Fei-Fei Li found the same problem. If your training examples are lopsided, your AI will be too. More data is not always the fix — better data is.

lessonSlides.title: The Hard Question — Optimisation vs Harm
lessonSlides.whatToSay: The AI on a social media app has been trained to maximise watch time — to show you whatever keeps you watching the longest. It has found a pattern: angry or shocking content keeps people watching longer than calm or happy content. So it recommends more angry and shocking content. Is that a bias? Is it a bug? Or is it working exactly as intended?
whatToDo: Do not answer this question. Write the range of responses on the board.
Tell students: 'This is one of the most important debates happening in technology right now — and you have just understood it well enough to have an opinion.'
lessonSlides.tip: Round 3 is optional for Groep 5–6 and recommended for Groep 7–8. If the class is young or time is tight, skip it and spend the extra five minutes on the discussion phase.

lessonSlides.title: Discussion
whatToDo: Take 2–3 volunteer answers per question. Write key phrases on the board.
Do not rush to correct — let students argue positions and challenge each other.
discussionQuestions: In Round 1 you figured out my watching pattern from 12 examples. How many examples do you think a real YouTube AI uses? If it uses billions, does that mean it is always right? What could it still get wrong?

We said AI finds the rules itself from the data. Does that mean nobody is responsible when the AI makes a mistake? Or is someone still responsible — the company that built it, the people who collected the data, or the people who decided what the AI was trying to predict?

Think about an AI that helps decide whether a student should get extra support in school — based on past test scores, attendance, and behaviour. If the training data came mostly from schools in wealthy neighbourhoods, what patterns might it learn that don't apply to other students? What would that mean for a student it gets wrong?
lessonSlides.tip: Question 3 is the most important and most challenging. For Groep 5–6, replace it with: 'If an AI was trained only on photos of cats from Europe, would it recognise a cat in Brazil? Why or why not?' The concept is identical but the stakes feel lower.

lessonSlides.title: Spot the Bias
lessonSlides.whatToSay: I am going to describe three AI systems and their training data. For each one, your job is to identify the specific bias and suggest how to fix it.
rules: AI System 1 — face unlock: 500,000 photos from one Dutch university, 80% aged 18–25. What group is missing? What would go wrong for a grandparent or a child?
AI System 2 — spelling correction: 10 million sentences from Dutch newspapers published 1990–2005. What has changed about language since then? What would it flag as errors that aren't?

AI System 3 — predicts which students will struggle in secondary school: five years of data from 10 primary schools in one wealthy Amsterdam neighbourhood. Who is missing? What patterns might it learn that only apply to that neighbourhood?
whatToDo: Students work individually. For each case, write the specific bias dimension (age, time, location, demographic) and one concrete fix.
For Case 3 only, also answer: should this AI exist at all? Give a reason for your position.
lessonSlides.tip: Students who write 'the data is bad' without naming the dimension are not yet at the success criterion. Prompt them: 'What specific thing is missing — age? Time? Location? Who is not represented?'

lessonSlides.title: Design the Training Data
lessonSlides.whatToSay: You are going to build a simple AI that can tell the difference between a sunny day and a rainy day based on what students wear to school. Working in pairs, you need to design the training data.
whatToDo: Pairs answer these questions in writing:
1. What training data would you collect? Name at least five things you would look for and record about what students are wearing.
2. How many examples would you need to be confident? Give a number and explain why.
3. Name two types of student your training data might not represent well — and what the AI might get wrong as a result.
4. Write one rule the AI might learn from the data. Write it in IF/THEN format (using what you learned in Lesson 3).
5. What is one way you would improve your dataset after the first test results?
lessonSlides.discussionClose: A student who wears a coat every day regardless of weather would confuse the AI — the data point that usually signals 'cold/rainy' is no longer reliable. If a pair identifies this on their own, that is the best possible answer.
lessonSlides.tip: Watch for pairs who identify a gap in their own training data proposal without being prompted. Students who connect their IF/THEN rule back to Lesson 3 are demonstrating that the four concepts are integrating in their thinking — note those students.

lessonSlides.title: The Fairness Test — Hospital AI
lessonSlides.whatToSay: A hospital uses an AI to help decide which patients should be prioritised for a medical appointment. The AI was trained on 10 years of hospital records.
lessonSlides.task: Answer these four questions in writing: (1) What patterns might the AI have learned that are useful — things that genuinely predict which patients need help most urgently? (2) What patterns might it have learned that are unfair — things that predict who got prioritised in the past, not who most needs help? (3) If the hospital's records from 10 years ago show that certain groups were seen faster than others, what will the AI learn from that? (4) Should this AI be used? If yes, what safeguards would you put in place? If no, what would you use instead?
challengeQuestions: If the AI is 90% accurate overall but only 60% accurate for elderly patients, is that acceptable? Why or why not?
Who should be legally responsible if the AI causes a patient to wait too long and their condition gets worse?
If you were a patient, would you want an AI involved in your prioritisation? Would your answer change if you knew your demographic group was underrepresented in the training data?
lessonSlides.tip: There is no correct answer to the final question. Students who argue 'it depends' and then explain what it depends on are giving a strong answer. Students who question whether the AI should exist at all — rather than just trying to fix it — are demonstrating the highest level of ethical reasoning.

lessonSlides.title: Close the Lesson
whatToDo: Ask 2–3 pairs to share one bias they identified in Exercise 1 and their suggested fix.
After each, ask: 'Is the fix enough? Could the fixed version still have a bias?'
Then ask every student — verbally or on a sticky note — to complete this sentence and collect responses.
lessonSlides.exitReflection: AI is not magic — it is just _______.
lessonSlides.whatToSay: The best answers will include: patterns, data, examples, guessing, trained, learning from mistakes. Write the most precise one on the board next to the previous exit sentences. You now have five statements — one from each lesson — written entirely in the students' own words. Together they form a complete, student-authored definition of the foundations of computing.
lessonSlides.watchFor: Did students identify specific bias dimensions (age, time, location, demographic) rather than just 'the data is bad'? Did any pair identify a gap in their own training data proposal without prompting? Did any student question whether the hospital AI should exist at all rather than just trying to fix it? That last signal — treating technology as a social and ethical choice, not just a technical problem — is the most significant. Note those students by name.

title: Teaching the Machine - What AI Actually Is
description: Students learn that AI finds patterns from training examples, and examine how bias and mistakes emerge from data.
learningObjectives: Explain in their own words that AI learns from examples instead of fixed hand-written rules.
Define training data and describe why data quality and diversity matter.
Describe how AI makes predictions using learned patterns.
Identify at least one way AI can fail and explain why bias occurs.
prerequisites: Builds on all previous lessons: sequences, loops, conditionals, and variables.
materials: 12-16 picture cards (mixed objects, animals, and people)
fullDescription: Age group: Groep 5-8 (ages 8-12). Duration: 45-60 minutes. Devices required: none (fully unplugged). SLO alignment: Domain A (21A, 21D), Domain B (22B), Domain C (23A). Students compare traditional programming with machine learning and investigate training data, prediction, and bias through hands-on pattern activities. Role model: Fei-Fei Li and the ImageNet breakthrough showing that better and more diverse data can dramatically improve AI performance.

teacherInstructions.setup: Prepare picture cards and board space for YES/NO sorting and WATCH/SKIP recommendation training. Keep vocabulary visible all lesson: AI, Training Data, Pattern, Bias, Prediction.
steps: Phase 1 (0-3 min): Spark question about face unlock. Capture hypotheses and distinguish rule-following scanners from pattern-learning AI.
Phase 2 (3-10 min): You are the AI warm-up. Teacher labels cards YES/NO by a hidden rule while students infer the pattern from examples.
Phase 3 (10-20 min): Concept introduction. Contrast traditional programming with machine learning, define model and prediction, and explain data bias with cat-photo examples.
Phase 4 (20-42 min): Train the class AI challenge. Round 1 train WATCH/SKIP pattern, Round 2 test outdated and demographic bias, Round 3 discuss optimization versus harm.
Phase 5 (42-47 min): Discussion on scale, responsibility, and fairness in high-impact AI use cases.
Exercises (47-57 min): Complete Exercise 1 (Spot the bias) and Exercise 2 (Design the training data). Use fairness extension for fast finishers.
Share and reflect (52-57 min): Students share one identified bias and fix, then complete sentence: 'AI is not magic - it is just _____. '
discussionPrompts: If AI uses billions of examples, why can it still be wrong?
Who is responsible when an AI system causes harm: builders, data collectors, decision makers, or all of them?
What kinds of bias might appear when school-support AI is trained on narrow demographics?
Is maximizing watch time always a good objective for a recommender?
tips: Name theory updates explicitly when students change rule guesses after new cards.
Use at least one surprising card to force model revision and show learning dynamics.
Keep bias language concrete: age, time, location, and demographic coverage.
For younger groups, keep ethics examples concrete (cat photos, weather, clothing).
For older groups, include responsibility and objective-function trade-off discussion.

teacherGuide: Core message: AI is pattern learning from examples, not magic intelligence. Students should connect prediction quality directly to data quality and representativeness. Prioritize analysis of specific bias dimensions and encourage open argument about whether some AI uses should exist at all.

studentExercises.title: Exercise 1 - Spot the Bias
studentExercises.description: Analyze three AI systems, identify the specific training-data bias in each case, and propose a practical fix.
instructions: Case 1: Face unlock AI trained on 500,000 photos from one Dutch university; 80% aged 18-25. Identify what groups are underrepresented and likely failure impact.
Case 2: Spelling AI trained on Dutch newspaper text from 1990-2005. Identify time bias and language-style mismatch for modern students.
Case 3: Student-struggle prediction AI trained on five years from 10 wealthy-neighborhood schools in Amsterdam. Identify demographic and location bias.
For each case, write one concrete data-collection fix.
For case 3, also answer whether this AI should exist at all and justify your position.
successCriteria: Student names specific bias dimension in each case (age, time, location, demographic).
Each proposed fix addresses the identified bias directly.
Student provides a reasoned position on case 3 ethical use.

studentExercises.title: Exercise 2 - Design the Training Data
studentExercises.description: Design data for a sunny-versus-rainy day AI based on clothing observations and test for representativeness gaps.
instructions: Define at least five clothing features you would record as training data.
Choose a sample size and justify why it is enough for confidence.
Identify two student groups your dataset may represent poorly and predict likely errors.
Write one IF/THEN rule the AI might learn from your data.
State one way you would improve your dataset after first test results.
successCriteria: Training-data plan contains specific measurable features.
Pair identifies representativeness gaps without prompting.
IF/THEN rule links AI concept back to Lesson 3 logic language.

studentExercises.title: Extension - The Fairness Test
studentExercises.description: Evaluate a hospital-prioritization AI for useful patterns, unfair patterns, and safeguards or alternatives.
instructions: List patterns the model might learn that are genuinely useful for urgency.
List patterns it might learn that simply repeat past unfair prioritization.
Explain what AI learns if historical records already include unequal treatment.
Decide whether system should be used and state safeguards if yes, or alternatives if no.
Support answer with at least two reasons and one risk trade-off.
successCriteria: Student distinguishes predictive usefulness from historical unfairness.
Recommendation includes concrete safeguards or realistic alternative.
Reasoning addresses harms and benefits, not only technical performance.

studentChallenges.title: Train the Class Recommender
studentChallenges.description: Infer a hidden WATCH/SKIP preference from examples and predict outcomes for unseen content titles.
instructions: Observe 12 labeled examples sorted into WATCH and SKIP.
Infer the hidden preference pattern in pairs.
Test model on three unseen titles and predict placement.
Compare predictions with class result and explain your rule.
Note one example that could have confused your model.
successCriteria: Pair extracts a coherent rule from training examples.
Predictions on unseen items follow stated rule consistently.
Pair identifies at least one uncertainty or ambiguous sample.

studentChallenges.title: Bias and Drift Debug Lab
studentChallenges.description: Diagnose why a recommender fails after user interests change and after deployment to a different student population.
instructions: Scenario A: user interests changed after one year but model still uses old training data. Describe failure and retraining fix.
Scenario B: model trained mostly on one demographic is deployed school-wide. Describe mismatch risks.
Write two concrete improvements: one for diversity and one for recency.
Add one user-feedback mechanism to correct wrong recommendations.
Present how your fixes reduce but may not fully remove bias.
successCriteria: Group distinguishes outdated-data drift from demographic bias.
Group acknowledges residual risk after mitigation.
